sources.notes: Statement and constraints: decide reachability between two binary strings under recursive half-addition operations.
Official editorial for Codeforces Round 1021; used to confirm the row-operation and bitset linear-basis solution.
Independent writeup cross-checks the block matrix interpretation and row-basis equivalence.
Chinese writeup confirms decomposing n=m*2^k and comparing the linear bases of the 2^k rows of length m.

annotation.summary: Determine whether one binary string can be transformed into another by recursively adding one half of a block to the other half modulo 2.
annotation.constraints: Sum n <= 1000000; the solution needs near-linear or bitset-optimized linear algebra over GF(2).
annotation.core_idea: Write n = 2^k * m with m odd and view the string as a matrix with 2^k rows and m columns. The allowed operation on a block corresponds to adding one row-block to another inside a recursive binary decomposition. Using swaps built from three additions and induction over the decomposition, these operations generate arbitrary elementary row operations among the 2^k rows. Therefore s can become t if and only if the row spaces of the two matrices over GF(2) are equal. Compute a canonical XOR linear basis or row-reduced form for both matrices and compare.
annotation.complexity: O(2^k * min(2^k,m) * m / word_size) with bitset Gaussian elimination
tricks: Factor n into an odd block length times a power of two.
Reinterpret recursive half operations as elementary row operations.
Compare row spaces via a canonical XOR basis.

solution_variants.name: gf2-row-space-comparison
solution_variants.summary: Split each string into 2^k rows of length m, compute a bitset Gaussian-elimination basis for both row sets, and compare canonical bases.

tags.evidence: Reachability is exactly equality of the GF(2) row spaces spanned by the block rows of s and t.
The implementation computes canonical XOR bases of the row vectors and compares them.
The decisive representation is a binary matrix whose rows can undergo elementary row operations.
Rows are stored as bitsets/bitmasks to make Gaussian elimination fast enough.
